editor_note: The prose is genuinely native, fluent Romanian technical writing — not machine translation. Analogies (the flipped coin "atât cu fața, cât și cu reversul în sus", the guitar chord, the two-scarf entanglement metaphor) are rendered with full pedagogical clarity and the same intuition as the English; coin terms "cap"/"pajură" are the correct conventional choices; informal "tu" register holds consistently (poți, gândește-te, reamintește-ți, "ești liber(ă)") with no dumneavoastră slips. Meaning is faithful throughout — no inverted negations, swapped quantities, softened claims, or dropped caveats. The one real defect is a within-file inconsistency on the core term "superposition": it is rendered THREE ways — "superpozițiune/superpozițiunea" in the definition section and its heading (lines 49, 78, 80, 82), then "suprapunere/suprapunerea" from line 83 onward (lines 83, 95, 97, 99, 103, 105), then "superpoziție" in the Key Takeaways (lines 173–174). Each form is individually defensible, but mixing all three in one teaching file risks a learner not recognizing them as the same concept. "Entanglement" is, by contrast, kept consistently as an English loanword throughout (with the mildly awkward coined participle "entanglate" at line 49), which is an acceptable, consistent convention. This is an isolated terminology-consistency slip that does not mislead, hence MINOR rather than FAIL.

examples.source: the quantum phenomena of superposition... / A system of n qubits... / A quantum superposition can have exponentially more states...
examples.translation: superpozițiunii (l.41/49/80) ... suprapunerea/suprapunere (l.83/95/97/99/103/105) ... superpoziție (l.173/174)
examples.suggested: Pick one rendering of "superposition" and use it throughout — e.g. standardize on "suprapunere" for the heading, definition, body, and takeaways.
examples.why: The single English term "superposition" appears as three distinct Romanian words within one file (superpozițiune / suprapunere / superpoziție). All are defensible, but a learner meeting "superpozițiune" in the definition and "suprapunerea" two paragraphs later may not realize it is the same core concept being taught.

examples.source: ...are entangled—they are states of the whole computer...
examples.translation: sunt entanglate — ele sunt stări ale întregului calculator...
examples.suggested: ...sunt în stare de entanglement — ...
examples.why: "entanglate" is a coined Romanian past participle from the English loanword; understandable and consistent with the file's choice to keep "entanglement" in English, but reads slightly awkwardly. Minor; the loanword convention itself is consistently applied.

editor_note: The prose is, on the whole, fluent, idiomatic Romanian with the correct informal "tu" register held throughout and good physics phrasing ("câmp magnetic neomogen", "moment de torsiune/cuplu", "condiția de normalizare", "stare proprie", "colapsul stării"). It reads like a real Romanian physics writer for most of the file, which is why this is a near-miss rather than a clear failure. However, three distinct within-file terminology inconsistencies push it to FAIL. (1) "shots" is rendered three incompatible ways: as "iterații" (number of iterations) in the second experiment (lines 366, 398), left untranslated as "shot" at line 427, while the code variable stays num_shots — "iterații" is the wrong domain term for shots and breaks the link to the num_shots variable the text discusses. (2) "gate" appears as the loanword "gate"/"Gate" (lines 485, 756, the latter wrongly capitalized mid-sentence) but elsewhere correctly as the native "poartă/porți" (lines 288, 404). (3) "angular momentum" is the conventional Romanian "moment cinetic" in the Background section but switches to the calque "moment unghiular" at lines 401, 484, 532. There are also two outright local errors: an untranslated English word "particle" inside a Romanian sentence at line 236 ("un singur particle"), and a gender-agreement error "are mulți particule" at line 121 (should be "multe particule"). None of these inverts the physics, so the file does not mislead a reader, but the recurring term-splits and the shots→iterații choice are exactly the kind of defects a domain editor must reject.

This is largely faithful, fluent Thai technical prose that follows the standard doQumentation convention of leaving core domain terms in English (transpile/transpilation, qubit, gate, circuit, backend, layout, routing, SWAP, pass manager, stochastic) and binding them with idiomatic Thai connectives — phrases like "ทำงานน้อยมากตามค่าเริ่มต้น" and "ต้องต่อสู้กับ depth ที่เพิ่มขึ้นจากขั้นตอน layout และ routing" read the way a Thai quantum engineer would actually write, and terminology is used consistently throughout (no within-file term collisions; informal register holds, no ครับ/ค่ะ). The two real slips are both naturalness, not meaning: (1) at line 154 "As a product of three CNOT gates" is calqued as "ในฐานะผลิตภัณฑ์ของ CNOT gate สาม gate" — "ผลิตภัณฑ์" is the commercial "manufactured product" sense, jarring for the compositional/mathematical "product"; a native writer would say "เนื่องจากประกอบด้วย/เกิดจาก CNOT gate สามตัว". (2) "noise/noisy" drifts between Thai "สัญญาณรบกวน" (lines 67, 154, 166) and English "noisy devices" (line 169) — understandable but inconsistent. No semantic drift, negations and quantities are preserved, examples still match their setups, and the pedagogical emphasis is intact, so this is MINOR, not FAIL.

examples.source: As a product of three CNOT gates, a SWAP is an expensive operation
examples.translation: ในฐานะผลิตภัณฑ์ของ CNOT gate สาม gate SWAP จึงเป็นการดำเนินการที่มีต้นทุนสูง
examples.suggested: เนื่องจากประกอบด้วย CNOT gate สามตัว SWAP จึงเป็นการดำเนินการที่มีต้นทุนสูง
examples.why: "ผลิตภัณฑ์" is the commercial 'manufactured product' sense and reads as raw MT; the English 'product' here means 'composed of / made up of' three CNOTs, which Thai expresses with ประกอบด้วย/เกิดจาก.

examples.source: noisy operation ... noisy devices
examples.translation: การดำเนินการที่มีสัญญาณรบกวน (L67) vs. noisy devices (L169)
examples.suggested: choose one rendering for noise across the file (either Thai สัญญาณรบกวน throughout or English noisy throughout)
examples.why: 'noise/noisy' is translated to Thai in some passages and left in English in others; not misleading but an avoidable within-file inconsistency.

editor_note: This reads like native, well-edited Thai technical prose, not raw MT: the translator confidently keeps field-conventional terms in English near code and in formulas (lattice, basis, modulus, SVP/CVP/BDD/LWE, KEM, IND-CPA/IND-CCA, Module-LWE/Ring-LWE, public/private key) while translating conceptual vocabulary correctly and consistently (การเข้ารหัส, คีย์สมมาตร/อสมมาตร, การแยกตัวประกอบเฉพาะ = prime factorization, ความยากในกรณีเฉลี่ย/กรณีเลวร้ายที่สุด = average/worst-case hardness, ลายเซ็นดิจิทัล). The teaching analogies (หอไอเฟล, เพชร/lattice, อิฐก่อสร้างของเล่น/basis) are rendered naturally and explain as clearly as the English; register is consistently neutral-casual with no polite particles ครับ/ค่ะ. One genuine defect: line 117 has a doubled-negation typo "basis...นั้นไม่ไม่เหมือนกัน" for "the basis for a given lattice is not unique" — read literally this is "not not-unique" = "is unique," inverting the claim and contradicting the very next clause that two bases can describe the same lattice (a native reader will likely self-correct, but it is a real error). Smaller slips: "discrete logarithm" appears once as the half-calqued "logarithm แบบไม่ต่อเนื่อง" yet is left fully in English "discrete logarithm" twice (lines 35, 53), and public key alternates between English and คีย์สาธารณะ — both minor and non-misleading.

examples.source: Importantly, the basis for a given lattice is not unique.
examples.translation: ที่สำคัญ basis สำหรับ lattice ที่กำหนดนั้นไม่ไม่เหมือนกัน
examples.suggested: ที่สำคัญ basis สำหรับ lattice ที่กำหนดนั้นไม่ได้มีเพียงชุดเดียว
examples.why: Doubled negation 'ไม่ไม่' (not-not) literally inverts the meaning to 'is unique,' the opposite of the English, and contradicts the following clause that two different bases describe the same lattice. The phrasing 'ไม่เหมือนกัน' (not the same) is also weaker than 'not unique'; 'ไม่ได้มีเพียงชุดเดียว' captures non-uniqueness precisely.

examples.source: the prime factoring and discrete logarithm problems
examples.translation: logarithm แบบไม่ต่อเนื่อง
examples.suggested: discrete logarithm
examples.why: Half-calqued rendering (English 'logarithm' + Thai 'แบบไม่ต่อเนื่อง') is inconsistent with the same term being left fully in English at lines 43 and 53; the field standard in Thai writing keeps 'discrete logarithm' untranslated. Use one consistent form throughout the file.

editor_note: This is fluent, natural Thai technical prose that reads as if written by a Thai-speaking quantum engineer, not machine output — connectives like "พูดง่าย ๆ คือ", "มาสำรวจเรื่องนี้กัน", and "ลองนึกถึง" flow idiomatically, and the casual register (no ครับ/ค่ะ) holds consistently throughout. Terminology is consistent and well-judged: ความไม่แน่นอน (uncertainty), ค่าความคาดหวัง (expectation value), ความแปรปรวน (variance), การฉาย (projection), ตัวดำเนินการ (operator), สับเปลี่ยน/คอมมิวเตเตอร์ (commute/commutator), while correctly keeping field-standard English for Qubit, eigenstate, observable, Sampler, Estimator, backend, Gate, Circuit. The reason this is MINOR rather than PASS is a real wrong-word that recurs: "เปลี่ยนโค้ง" (line 894) and "แก้ไขโค้งข้างต้น" (line 916) render "Change/Modify the code" but โค้ง means "curve/bend" — it should be โค้ด (code); a single-character slip that produces nonsense ("change the curve") in instructional sentences. Separately, "satisfied" in "the uncertainty relation could be satisfied" is rendered as "สมดุลได้" (be balanced/saturated) at lines 606 and 662, a mild semantic softening — the relation being satisfied means the inequality holds, not that it is saturated. None of these mislead a reader badly, but the โค้ง/โค้ด typo should be corrected.

examples.source: Change the code or copy it over... / Modify the code above to make a similar picture
examples.translation: เปลี่ยนโค้งหรือคัดลอกมา... / แก้ไขโค้งข้างต้น
examples.suggested: เปลี่ยนโค้ดหรือคัดลอกมา... / แก้ไขโค้ดข้างต้น
examples.why: "โค้ง" means curve/bend, not code. The source says "the code"; the correct word is "โค้ด". A single-character typo that turns an instruction to edit the code into "change the curve" — a native reader notices it as wrong.

examples.source: So the uncertainty relation could be satisfied. / it is plausible that the uncertainty relation could again be satisfied.
examples.translation: ดังนั้นความสัมพันธ์ความไม่แน่นอนจึงสามารถสมดุลได้ / เป็นไปได้ที่ความสัมพันธ์ความไม่แน่นอนจะยังคงสมดุลได้อีกครั้ง
examples.suggested: ...จึงสามารถเป็นจริงได้ / ...จะยังคงเป็นจริงได้อีกครั้ง
examples.why: "satisfied" here means the inequality holds, but "สมดุลได้" reads as balanced/saturated (equality reached). Mild softening/shift of the claim; เป็นจริง/เป็นไปตาม is more faithful.

examples.source: in order to know $where$ a wave-like particle is located
examples.translation: เพื่อจะรู้ $where$ ว่าอนุภาคที่มีลักษณะเหมือนคลื่นอยู่ที่ไหน
examples.suggested: เพื่อจะรู้ว่าอนุภาคที่มีลักษณะเหมือนคลื่นอยู่ที่ไหน
examples.why: The literal English token "$where$" (an italic emphasis artifact from the source) is left embedded in Thai prose that already says "...อยู่ที่ไหน". Harmless and inherited from the source, but the bare English word looks awkward to a Thai reader.

editor_note: This reads as fluent, natural technical Tagalog written by someone comfortable in the domain, not raw MT. Domain nouns the field keeps in English (job, batch, session, QPU, TTL, backend, timeout, max_execution_time, wall clock time, QPU usage) are correctly left untranslated and used consistently throughout, while the connective prose is idiomatic Tagalog ("Para masiguro ang pagiging patas", "ang pinakamaliit sa mga halagang ito", "ginagamit ang mas maliit sa dalawang halaga"). Meaning is faithful in every paragraph: the cap of "3 oras", the 5000/83-minuto vs 5-minuto/300-segundo example, "put into a failed state" → "ilalagay sa failed na estado", and "run to completion" → "patuloy na tatakbo hanggang sa matapos" all preserve the English exactly. Register is consistently informal (no po/opo). The only soft spot is L60, where "interactive TTL ... between jobs that cannot be configured" becomes "na halaga sa pagitan ng mga job na hindi maaaring i-configure," letting "hindi maaaring i-configure" appear to attach to "job" rather than the TTL — an inherited ambiguity from the awkward source that context still resolves; not misleading.

examples.source: Batches also have an interactive time to live (interactive TTL) value between jobs that cannot be configured.
examples.translation: Ang mga batch ay mayroon ding interactive time to live (interactive TTL) na halaga sa pagitan ng mga job na hindi maaaring i-configure.
examples.suggested: Ang mga batch ay mayroon ding interactive time to live (interactive TTL) na halaga sa pagitan ng mga job, at ang halagang ito ay hindi maaaring i-configure.
examples.why: The relative clause 'na hindi maaaring i-configure' grammatically attaches to 'mga job' (jobs), but the English means the interactive TTL itself cannot be configured. Recoverable from context, so MINOR, but rephrasing makes the referent unambiguous.

editor_note: This reads as well-edited, fluent Tagalog technical prose, not raw MT. The translator makes the correct, consistent convention choice of keeping core domain terms in English with Tagalog affixation where natural ("quantum state," "density matrix/matrices," "unitary matrix," "eigenvalue," "naka-entangle" for entangled), and renders structural vocabulary idiomatically ("framework" → "balangkas," "noteworthy advantages" → "kapansin-pansing bentahe," "we wish to ignore" → "nais nating balewalain"). Register is correctly informal throughout (natin/nating, sisimulan nating tuklasin) with no po/opo slips, and meaning is faithful with no semantic drift, inverted conditions, or dropped caveats. Two minor, non-misleading slips: (1) "noise" is translated as "ingay" (lines 24, 29), the everyday audible-sound word, where the quantum-computing field conventionally keeps "noise" in English — it is consistent and understandable but reads slightly less like a domain expert; (2) the general-present English "describe" is rendered with past-tense "naglarawan" twice (lines 35, 39: "ang mga unitary matrix ay naglarawan," "ang mga eigenvalue... ay naglarawan") where a stative/present form (kumakatawan / naglalarawan) would better match the timeless general claim. Neither error changes meaning or pedagogy; the intuition and emphasis match the English.

examples.source: model the effects of noise on quantum computations
examples.translation: i-modelo ang mga epekto ng ingay sa mga quantum computation
examples.suggested: i-modelo ang mga epekto ng noise sa mga quantum computation
examples.why: "ingay" is the everyday word for audible sound; the quantum-computing field conventionally keeps "noise" in English. Used consistently (also line 29 "nakalantad sa ingay") so it does not mislead, but reads slightly less like a domain expert wrote it.

examples.source: unitary matrices describe quantum operations ... and stochastic matrices describe probabilistic operations
examples.translation: ang mga unitary matrix ay naglarawan ng mga quantum operation ... at ang mga stochastic matrix ay naglarawan ng mga probabilistik na operasyon
examples.suggested: ang mga unitary matrix ay naglalarawan ng ... at ang mga stochastic matrix ay naglalarawan ng ...
examples.why: English uses the general present tense for a timeless fact; "naglarawan" is past tense. A native technical writer would use the present/stative "naglalarawan" or "kumakatawan." Recurs at line 39 ("ang mga eigenvalue ... ay naglarawan"). Minor; meaning is preserved.

editor_note: Overall this is fluent, well-paced teaching Tagalog with a consistent informal register and a sound terminology strategy: domain terms (qubit, gate, circuit, superposition, entanglement, transpiler, backend, Sampler, truth table) are correctly kept in English while connective prose is genuinely idiomatic, so most of the file reads as written by a Tagalog-speaking engineer, not MT. However there is one real semantic reversal that fails the file: at the multi-qubit-gates paragraph (TL line 409) the English "[classical gates] which lose information and are irreversible" is rendered "na nagtatambak ng impormasyon" — "nagtatambak" means pile up/accumulate, the exact opposite of "lose information," and this is a load-bearing teaching point about reversibility. Separately, the verb "collapse" (measurement collapsing the state) is rendered three different ways and two are malformed Tagalog morphology: "magbu-bumagsak" (line 549) and "Binubumagsak" (line 642) are non-standard reduplications of bumagsak, while the same idea is elsewhere "bumubuwag/binubuwag" (lines 804/824) — inconsistent and partly ungrammatical for a core concept. Minor supporting issues: "Measurement"/"Backend"/"Transpiler" are erratically capitalized mid-sentence, and "key resource" becomes the non-word "mahalagang sangkukunan" (line 551). The capitalization and "sangkukunan" alone would be MINOR, but the reversed "lose information" claim plus the malformed/triple-rendered "collapse" verb push this to FAIL.

examples.source: This stands in contrast to the classical gates we saw above such as AND or OR, which lose information and are irreversible
examples.translation: Ito ay kabaliktaran ng mga klasikal na gate na nakita natin sa itaas, tulad ng AND o OR, na nagtatambak ng impormasyon at hindi nare-reverse
examples.suggested: ...na nawawalan ng impormasyon at hindi nare-reverse
examples.why: 'nagtatambak ng impormasyon' = pile up/accumulate information, the reverse of the source's 'lose information' — this is a meaning error on a key irreversibility teaching point.

examples.source: a measurement will collapse the state to one of the basis functions
examples.translation: isang measurement ang magbu-bumagsak ng estado sa isa sa mga basis function
examples.suggested: isang measurement ang magpapabagsak ng estado sa isa sa mga basis function
examples.why: 'magbu-bumagsak' is malformed reduplication; a native writer would use 'magpapabagsak'/'magbabagsak'. Same malformed form 'Binubumagsak' recurs at ~line 642.

examples.source: measurement collapses a quantum superposition state into one of its classically allowed states
examples.translation: Ang mga Measurement ay bumubuwag ng isang quantum superposition state...
examples.suggested: Pick one verb for 'collapse' throughout (e.g. consistently 'nagpapabagsak')
examples.why: 'collapse' is rendered three ways across the file (magbu-bumagsak / Binubumagsak / bumubuwag-binubuwag), creating within-file inconsistency for a central concept.

examples.source: It is in fact a key resource in certain algorithms and protocols
examples.translation: Ito ay isang mahalagang sangkukunan sa ilang mga algorithm at protocol
examples.suggested: Ito ay isang mahalagang yaman/rekurso sa ilang mga algorithm at protocol
examples.why: 'sangkukunan' is not a standard Tagalog word (apparent malformation of 'pinagkukunan'); reads as a typo to a native reader.

examples.source: backend / transpiler / measurement (lowercase common nouns in English)
examples.translation: Backend / Transpiler / Measurement capitalized mid-sentence
examples.suggested: backend, transpiler, measurement (lowercase)
examples.why: Erratic mid-sentence capitalization of common technical nouns is inconsistent with the rest of the file and with the English; cosmetic but distracting.

editor_note: This reads as genuine native Ukrainian technical prose, not raw MT: domain terminology is conventional and consistent throughout — "вентиль" (gate), "схема" (circuit), "кубіт"/"двокубітних"/"однокубітних" (qubit/two-qubit/single-qubit), "транспілятор"/"транспіляція", "бекенд" (correctly kept, not over-translated), "точність" (fidelity), "унітарний оператор", and "KAK-розкладання Картана" for Cartan's KAK decomposition. The informal "ти" register holds cleanly across the whole file (твої, дивись, спробуй, почни, зверни увагу, продовж) with no formal slips, and all quantitative claims (six→six→three→two ECR gates, "не більш ніж трьома вентилями", 127→2 qubits, fidelity 1.000 vs 0.992) match the source exactly. Two small issues keep this from a clean PASS: in the optimization-level-0 table cell, "Basic translation" — the Qiskit basis-gate translation stage — is rendered as "Базовий переклад" (translation as in language/text), where a quantum engineer would expect "базова трансляція"; and line 195's relative clause "унітарним оператором, що реалізує ця схема" has slightly awkward word order (more natural: "який реалізує ця схема"). Neither misleads the reader, so this is MINOR.

examples.source: Basic translation
examples.translation: Базовий переклад
examples.suggested: Базова трансляція
examples.why: In the transpiler context "translation" is the basis-gate translation stage, not language translation; "переклад" reads as text/language translation. "Трансляція" is the conventional Ukrainian rendering of the Qiskit translation stage.

examples.source: the unitary operator this circuit implements
examples.translation: унітарним оператором, що реалізує ця схема
examples.suggested: унітарним оператором, який реалізує ця схема
examples.why: The relative clause word order with "що ... ця схема" is slightly stiff/ambiguous about subject; "який реалізує ця схема" flows more naturally for a native technical writer. Meaning is preserved, so this is a fluency nit only.

editor_note: This is genuinely well-edited Ukrainian technical prose, not raw MT — sentences are full and idiomatic, and the teaching intuition (circuit depth/noise, transpiled two-qubit depth, circuit cutting vs knitting, CLOPS-as-loose-analog-to-FLOPS, fragile non-cloneable qubit "memory") is conveyed as clearly as the English. Core domain terminology is correct and consistent: кубіт, гейт, схема, заплутаність, суперпозиція, гамільтоніан, транспіляція, бекенд, час когерентності, вірність (fidelity), and notably "математичне сподівання" for expectation value and "спостережуване" for observable — all the conventional choices. The reason this is MINOR rather than PASS is the systematic rendering of QPU/CPU/GPU as coined Cyrillic abbreviations ЦП/ГП/КПП: ЦП and ГП are standard, but "КПП" for QPU is an over-translation that no Ukrainian quantum engineer uses (the field keeps "QPU"; КПП normally reads as "checkpoint"/"gearbox") — it is at least applied consistently throughout. Secondary slips: "interleaved allocation" calqued as "черезрядкового розподілу" (means roughly "interlinear", not "перемежований/чергований"); two formal-register leaks "у вашій… схемі" (L226) and "ваших… ГП" (L246) against an otherwise consistent informal "ти"; and the slightly non-native "вирівнювання дає чітко побачити" (L98). None of these mislead the reader or distort the source meaning.

examples.source: a new type of resource - a quantum resource ... QPU (Quantum Processing Unit)
examples.translation: Новий тип ресурсу: КПП (квантовий процесор)
examples.suggested: Новий тип ресурсу: QPU (квантовий процесор)
examples.why: QPU is coined as the Cyrillic abbreviation КПП and used file-wide. Ukrainian quantum practitioners keep "QPU" in Latin; КПП is non-idiomatic (reads as "checkpoint"/"gearbox"). Consistent but an over-translation of a term the field retains in English.

examples.source: Currently there is no interleaved allocation of mixed resources.
examples.translation: Наразі не існує черезрядкового розподілу змішаних ресурсів.
examples.suggested: Наразі немає перемежованого (почергового) розподілу змішаних ресурсів.
examples.why: "черезрядковий" is a literal calque meaning "interlinear/every-other-line" and does not convey "interleaved" in the scheduling sense; a native writer would use перемежований/почерговий.

examples.source: transpilation automatically maps qubits in your abstract quantum circuit ... for each of your (say 10) GPUs
examples.translation: ...зіставляє кубіти у вашій абстрактній квантовій схемі...; ...для кожного з ваших (скажімо, 10) ГП
examples.suggested: ...у твоїй абстрактній квантовій схемі...; ...для кожного з твоїх (скажімо, 10) ГП
examples.why: Two formal-second-person slips (вашій, ваших) against an otherwise consistent informal "ти" register used everywhere else (визнач, перевір, сплануй, тобі знадобиться).

examples.source: the alignment makes it easy to see that the circuit has:
examples.translation: вирівнювання дає чітко побачити, що схема має:
examples.suggested: вирівнювання дає змогу легко побачити, що схема має:
examples.why: "дає чітко побачити" is non-standard collocation; native phrasing is "дає змогу/дозволяє побачити".

editor_note: The narrative prose (intro, theory, the "faster-than-light" caveat, Critical concepts) is genuinely native and well-edited: idiomatic Ukrainian, informal "ти" held throughout, no semantic drift, analogies preserved — that part teaches as well as the English. The FAIL is driven by systematic, pervasive terminology inconsistency. Four core domain terms are each rendered two ways within this one file: "gate" appears correctly as "гейт" in the whole theory section (гейт Адамара, гейт CNOT, гейт $X$/$Z$/SWAP) but then reverts to the untranslated capitalized "Gate" from Experiment 1 onward ("додаткових Gate", "умовних $X$ і $Z$ Gate", "SWAP Gate", "Gate операції", "що більше Gate ти додаєш", "цей додатковий Gate"); "circuit" is left almost everywhere as the English "Circuit" embedded mid-sentence ("квантовий Circuit", "діаграмі Circuit", "більший Circuit") instead of the conventional "(квантова) схема", and even collides with itself at line 587 "оптимізувати наш схема" (broken agreement, should be "нашу схему"); "backend" is left as capitalized "Backend" ("вибрати Backend", "найменш завантажений Backend") rather than "бекенд"; and "qubit" leaks untranslated twice ("якщо qubit початково перебуває", "кубіт Боба (qubit 0...)") despite "кубіт" being used consistently everywhere else. These capitalized English nouns dropped into otherwise-fluent Ukrainian sentences read as raw machine output and far exceed the three within-file inconsistencies that define a FAIL.

examples.source: the more gates you have, especially multi-qubit gates like swap gates, the more noise and errors you will encounter
examples.translation: що більше Gate ти використовуєш, особливо багатокубітних, як-от SWAP Gate, то більше шуму й помилок ти отримаєш
examples.suggested: що більше гейтів ти використовуєш, особливо багатокубітних, як-от гейти SWAP, то більше шуму й помилок ти отримаєш
examples.why: "gate" was rendered "гейт" consistently through the entire theory section, but from Experiment 1 onward it flips to the untranslated capitalized English "Gate". Same term, two renderings in one file — the defining within-file inconsistency.

examples.source: A circuit with a Hadamard gate is made as follows
examples.translation: Circuit із гейтом Адамара будується так
examples.suggested: Схема з гейтом Адамара будується так
examples.why: "circuit" is left as the English word "Circuit" capitalized inside a Ukrainian sentence (recurs as "квантовий Circuit", "діаграмі Circuit", "більший Circuit"). The conventional Ukrainian domain term is "(квантова) схема"; the English noun reads as raw MT.

examples.source: If we ask the pass manager to optimize our circuit, it will realize there is no logical reason for these swap gates
examples.translation: Якщо попросити менеджер проходів оптимізувати наш схема, він зрозуміє, що логічної причини для цих SWAP Gate немає
examples.suggested: Якщо попросити менеджер проходів оптимізувати нашу схему, він зрозуміє, що логічної причини для цих гейтів SWAP немає
examples.why: Grammatical agreement is broken: "наш схема" mixes masculine determiner with the feminine noun "схема" (should be "нашу схему"). This is the one place "circuit" was half-translated, exposing the inconsistency, plus "SWAP Gate" again untranslated.

examples.source: We must also select a backend on which to run... choose the least busy backend, or select a specific backend
examples.translation: Також потрібно вибрати Backend... обрати найменш завантажений Backend, або вибрати конкретний Backend
examples.suggested: Також потрібно вибрати бекенд... обрати найменш завантажений бекенд, або вибрати конкретний бекенд
examples.why: "backend" is left as capitalized English "Backend" three times in running prose. The established Ukrainian rendering is "бекенд" (Cyrillic); the bare English noun is translationese.

examples.source: if a qubit is initially in the state... ; Bob's qubit (qubit 0...)
examples.translation: якщо qubit початково перебуває у стані... ; кубіт Боба (qubit 0...)
examples.suggested: якщо кубіт початково перебуває у стані... ; кубіт Боба (кубіт 0...)
examples.why: "qubit" leaks untranslated twice while "кубіт" is used consistently in dozens of other places in the same file, including elsewhere in the very same sentences — a clear within-file inconsistency.
